content:
Convex analysis grew from 20th-century optimization and economics. **Jensen's inequality** (1906) formalized averaging behavior of convex functions. **John von Neumann** and **Oscar Morgenstern** used convex sets in game theory; **W. Fenchel** and **R. T. Rockafellar** developed modern convex analysis (epigraphs, subgradients, duality).

Classical ML relied on convex losses—least squares, hinge loss SVMs, logistic regression—where **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker** conditions and duality give global optimality certificates.

Deep learning revived interest in **non-convex** landscapes: researchers studied why simple gradient methods still work despite saddles (stochastic noise, overparameterization). The convex/non-convex distinction is now the standard language for explaining when optimization guarantees apply versus when heuristics dominate.

timeline.label: Convex analysis (mid 20th century)
timeline.description: Formal theory of convex sets and functions enables duality and global optimality certificates.

timeline.label: Convex ML era
timeline.description: SVMs, logistic regression, and Lasso exploit convexity so practitioners trust any found minimum.

timeline.label: Deep non-convex training
timeline.description: Large neural nets reintroduce rugged loss surfaces; empirical success drives research on saddles, flat minima, and SGD dynamics.
